kept.text:
Bitcoin just took back $80,000.

Most people will call this a breakout.

They’re wrong.

This isn’t price discovery…

it’s supply getting stripped from the market.

There’s less Bitcoin available now
than there was at $70K…
and even less than at $60K.

Every move higher is happening
with fewer coins left to buy.

This is how repricing begins.

Quiet… then violent.

kept.text:
🚨 SHOCKING DISCOVERY IN ASSAM 🚨

A live WWII-era bomb found buried in Assam’s Ledo-Lekhapani region has sent shockwaves across the area! 💣
What’s even more intense? 

This wasn’t just some rusted scrap — it was a fully unexploded ordnance (UXO), still dangerous after decades. One wrong move could’ve caused a massive disaster.

Thankfully, the Indian Army’s Bomb Disposal Squad stepped in like absolute pros 🇮🇳

They quickly secured the area, evacuated locals, and neutralized the bomb safely — preventing what could have been a tragedy.

Decades later, the scars of war still haunt us… but so does the courage of those who protect us.
Salute to our heroes 👏🔥

kept.text:
“Two days before the world changed, Honor Elizabeth Wainio was in Paris.
Friends called her Lizz. She was 27, a rising district manager for Discovery Channel Stores, just home from a long-awaited European trip. She had danced at a friend's wedding in Florence. She had walked the Champs-Élysées. She had slipped into a quiet church and lit a candle for her grandmother. Years earlier, she had told her mother that if she ever got to see Paris, she could die happy.
On the morning of September 11, 2001, she boarded United Flight 93 in Newark, headed to a company meeting in San Francisco. Forty souls were on that plane.
At 9:28 a.m., four hijackers seized the cockpit. Passengers were herded to the back. Through Airfones and cell calls, they began learning what had already happened in New York — and they understood, with terrible clarity, what their flight was meant to become.
At 9:53 a.m., Lizz called her stepmother, Esther Heymann.
Her voice was steady. Not panicked. Not broken. She had reached for the phone not to be comforted, but to comfort. "It just hurts me most," she said, "that this is going to be so much harder for you than it is for me."
They breathed together over the line. They prayed. And then Lizz said something her family has carried for twenty-five years. She told Esther: "I'm going to be with Grandma." Her grandmother had lived in rural Pennsylvania — not far, as it turned out, from a quiet field outside Shanksville. Lizz could not have known that. She only knew where her heart was pointing.
Around her, passengers had taken a vote. They had decided to fight.
Her last words came as the noise of struggle reached the back of the cabin: "I have to go. They're breaking into the cockpit. I love you."
Minutes later, Flight 93 came down in that Pennsylvania field — its target, almost certainly the U.S. Capitol, never reached. Forty ordinary people had refused to be passengers in their own ending.
Today, on the hill above the crash site, stands the Tower of Voices — forty wind chimes, each tuned to its own note. When the wind comes through, they don't sound the same. They sound like individuals.
Her father still listens for one particular chime.
What would you say, in four minutes, to the people you love most?”

kept.text:
April Recap | 2026
In April, we focused on product discovery, Agent use cases, and the long-term value of the creator ecosystem.

Weekly Showcase continued to highlight how creators use Agents across different scenarios, including visual transformation, photo restoration, identity changes, and video generation. From Ghibli Gram Filter and Old Photo Restoration to No Beard Filter and Earth Zoom Video Maker, creation continued to center on accessible tools with strong social sharing potential.

Our platform narrative also moved beyond individual tools. The discussion around AI Labor framed Agents as user-owned AI assets, where context, memory, and execution logic become part of a creator’s long-term value.

Growth-side updates added a more practical view of the ecosystem. Data around active creators, AI Agents, users, and AIPP transactions showed that MyShell’s creator economy is growing in scale while paying more attention to retention, discovery, and conversion.

MyShell continues to evolve as an AI consumer layer where creation, ownership, and distribution become more closely connected.

kept.text:
Massive reminder

AI just designed a brand-new antibiotic in seconds 👀

Traditional drug discovery = test 1 million molecules
SyntheMol-RL = explores up to 46 billion compounds

That’s a 4,600,000% increase in search power.

Researchers have built a generative AI system called SyntheMol-RL that can design novel antibiotic candidates from scratch.

Instead of painfully testing compounds one by one, the model builds molecules using real chemical reactions and 150,000 molecular building blocks.

Using reinforcement learning, it doesn’t just generate molecules, it optimizes them for antibacterial activity and water solubility at the same time.

In lab validation, the system: 
> Generated thousands of candidates
> Synthesized 79 real compounds
> Found 13 active antibiotics, including completely novel ones

One standout molecule, SYNTHECIN, showed strong effectiveness against drug-resistant MRSA in mouse infection models.

This is one of the first times an AI system has gone from idea To molecules To real-world validation this fast..

We went from searching for drugs to designing them on demand bro!

profile_bio.description: Ai, longevity, singularity, robotics, energy and latest SciTech breakthroughs, i bring it to you in a simple, easy-to-read feed ↘️ ! By @Alxom17

quoted_tweet.text:
Wow. AI just designed a brand-new antibiotic in seconds 👀

Traditional drug discovery = test 1 million molecules
SyntheMol-RL = explores up to 46 billion compounds

That’s a 4,600,000% increase in search power.

Researchers have built a generative AI system called SyntheMol-RL that can design novel antibiotic candidates from scratch.

Instead of painfully testing compounds one by one, the model builds molecules using real chemical reactions and 150,000 molecular building blocks.

Using reinforcement learning, it doesn’t just generate molecules, it optimizes them for antibacterial activity and water solubility at the same time.

In lab validation, the system: 
> Generated thousands of candidates
> Synthesized 79 real compounds
> Found 13 active antibiotics, including completely novel ones

One standout molecule, synthecin, showed strong effectiveness against drug-resistant Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) in mouse infection models.

This is one of the first times an AI system has gone from idea → molecule → real-world validation this fast.

We went from searching for drugs..
to designing them on demand.

kept.text: A big element of the ongoing updates to ‘Battle of Britain Then & Now’ from @penswordbooks lies in correcting details of the ‘minutiae’ of the battle. A case in point is revising the history of the Spitfire from which this artefact was recovered in 1974 and the discovery that its provenance and history is not as previously thought. That discovery led, in turn, to discovering that the history of the Battle of Britain Memorial flight’s Spitfire, P7350, was not as had long been thought.

kept.text: Back in 1972, space around Earth was almost silent.Only a few hundred satellites drifted overhead — quiet sentinels used mostly for weather watching, basic communications, and scientific discovery. The orbital realm was still vast and empty.Then everything changed.Launch costs plummeted. Technology exploded. Humanity’s hunger for connectivity and data surged. Satellite by satellite, constellation by constellation, we filled the https://t.co/w9BSNWzabc, in 2026, more than 11,700 active satellites orbit our planet. They form the invisible backbone of modern life: powering global internet and phone networks, guiding billions with GPS, forecasting deadly storms, monitoring climate change, and delivering high-resolution views of Earth in real time. Massive fleets now blanket the sky to keep every corner of the world connected.What was once an untouched celestial wilderness has become one of the busiest, most engineered regions in the Solar System — a human-made orbital ecosystem humming 24/7 above our https://t.co/HNGsvG56pW just over five decades, we went from a handful of lonely spacecraft to a dense, dynamic satellite layer that touches every aspect of life on the ground.This extraordinary growth is a testament to human ingenuity. But it also carries a clear message: the final frontier is no longer empty. How we manage it now — with responsibility, sustainability, and foresight — will define the next chapter of our journey into space.

The market is hot.
FOMO is real.
Price is in discovery mode.

The last time we caught the TROLL move from 10M to 180M from early monthly whale accumulation

There was 1 signal that caught the top at $180M (just 20% from the peak)

That signal, is FISH FLOWS

That’s retail money

And as we know too well, the exit liquidity 

Once retail net inflows on the weekly become weak, that’s the signal

Retail’s losing firepower at ATHs, who else is going to push the price in ATHs?

Nobody. So that’s your signal.

Right now that fish flow chart is parabolic, watch for the first daily decline.

kept.text:
Happy noon CT

Got some clarity on Arc Terminal

@TheARCTERMINAL is gradually becoming something bigger than just another AI tool

The idea isn’t to give you a chat with a model, it's to bring the entire workflow into one place - from information discovery to real on chain actions

At the center of it are agents. On ARC they don’t just respond to prompts, they execute task chains: find data, compare metrics, track conditions, and turn it into actual actions without breaking your flow

At the same time, a data and memory layer is being built. Through ARC Drive, context doesn’t get lost: actions, files, decisions, and history are structured into a system you can return to, and agents can build on it further

What you get isn’t a set of tools, it's an environment where thought turns into action faster, because there’s almost no gap between them

kept.text:
LLMs as scientific literature miners: a large language model guides the discovery of a new acidic OER catalyst

Green hydrogen from proton exchange membrane water electrolysis (PEMWE) is bottlenecked at the anode. Iridium is rare and costly. Ruthenium is more abundant and intrinsically more active, but dissolves under acidic, high-potential conditions. Ru-based high-entropy oxides offer a way out: mix five or more metals in near-equal ratios and let local disorder stabilize the structure. The catch is a search space of tens of thousands of quinary compositions.

Yuanhua Tu and coauthors combine an LLM with high-throughput experimentation. They retrieve about 1,400 papers on Ru-based OER from Web of Science and use GPT-4o, via a Python pipeline, for structured extraction over 13 stability and mechanism descriptors (metal-oxygen bond strength, oxygen-vacancy formation energy, Ru-O covalency). The LLM is not asked to predict performance; it extracts evidence snippets, the exact sentences in the source papers, aggregated into element-descriptor maps. Six emphasized elements (Co, Ni, Fe, Cu, Ti, Mo) and three potentially active ones (Cr, Zn, Sb) define a constrained 60-composition library.

The library is synthesized by rapid radiative heating to 1500 C and screened on an automated scanning flow cell with machine vision, around 100 samples per day. Screening uses dual metrics: kinetic activity and an early-stage degradation proxy. The intersection points to (RuNiFeMoCr)3O4: 236 mV at 10 mA/cm2, 96-fold higher mass activity than commercial RuO2, over 400 hours of bench stability, and more than 150 hours at 1 A/cm2 in a full PEMWE device.

Operando ICP-MS, infrared spectroscopy, isotope-labeled DEMS, and DFT on special quasi-random structures all converge: the high-entropy environment lowers Ru-O covalency, suppresses lattice oxygen participation, and shifts the reaction toward the more durable adsorbate evolution mechanism.

For energy materials, electrocatalysis, and clean hydrogen, the lesson goes beyond the catalyst itself. LLMs are earning their place not as oracles that predict properties, but as literature miners that turn fragmented experimental knowledge into structured, auditable design constraints. Paired with automated synthesis and screening, this is a credible recipe for compressing discovery cycles in compositionally complex materials problems.

Paper: Tu et al., Science Advances (2026) — CC BY-NC 4.0 | https://t.co/mkLO9qjj1G

kept.text:
The permanent DNA of science 

Imagine if every significant discovery about how to live longer and better lives was recorded in a book that could never be burnt, misplaced, or hidden behind a locked door.

Right Now, science doesn’t work that way.

The majority of research is renting its existence, living on private servers or behind pricey paywalls. 

Resting as in: if a corporation goes out of business or a website link fails, that knowledge or research associated may disappear or become hard to discover.

Cortex DeSci is addressing this by establishing a permanent record of human discoveries @CortexDesci 

                             How It Works 

⇛ Mining: People use a simple Chrome extension to extract details from difficult scientific papers 

⇛ The Blockchain: These facts are stored straight to Ethereum. Consider this to be the equivalent of carving information into digital stone

⇛ As a result, it becomes Permanent DNA since it is now an essential component of the internet that cannot be removed, filtered, or controlled by a single corporation

          The Goal ? A Map for Longevity

Cortex DeSci is concentrating this technology on Longevity research into anti aging and wellness. 

By participating in mining, you are not only collecting incentives, but also contributing to the creation of a large, permanent map of information that any researcher in the world may utilise to find treatments quicker.

We're transitioning science away from fragile files and secret directories. We're making it immortal.

50 years ago today, NASA launched a 2-foot (60-cm) ball that transformed studies of Earth’s shape, rotation, and gravity field. 

It has no sensors, no batteries, no electronics… just 426 retroreflectors that allow scientists to make extremely precise distance measurements using lasers.

LAGEOS is still orbiting Earth, and is expected to remain in its very stable orbit for millions of years!

kept.text:
On a trading floor you learn quickly that most activity is zero-sum. For every winner there's a loser. The money moves around but the total amount doesn't change.

That's fine and markets need that. Price discovery is valuable. But an economy that's only zero-sum has a ceiling.

For Base to change the world for the better the economy needs to be generative. It needs to create real value.

This is happening. Lending markets are creating credit. Stablecoin payments are replacing intermediary fees. Agent payments are expanding what's possible. Deep liqudity for majors like BTC and ETH. Stocks are coming onchain.

Trading on its own recirculates money. Trading inside a growing economy allocates capital to the things that are working. And that’s how value gets created.
